title: Key Validation
breadcrumbs: Validation at Field, Key, Occurrence, or Entity Level
text: Key Validation Uniface recognizes primary keys, candidate keys, and index keys. Uniface always validates primary keys, but validation of candidate and index keys depends on the declarative and procedural constraints. Candidate Keys When defining a candidate key, you can use the Validate property to determine how Uniface validates the candidate key at run time. If Validate is True , Uniface applies the same validation checks to the candidate key as are applied to primary keys. In forms, it activates the leaveModifiedKey trigger as appropriate in interactive sessions. If Validate is False , Uniface ignores the candidate key during the validateKey process. By default, Uniface set Validate to True for newly-defined candidate keys. Indexes Indexes in Uniface are used to improve performance when searching for data in a database. They do not have to be unique. Uniface does not apply specific validation checks to indexes, although the following key-related validation functions also apply to indexes: $keytype $keyfields Validating a Key Key validation can be initiated in the following ways: By an event that changes focus, such as going from a key field to a non-key field with the ^NEXT_FIELD structure editor function, prior to the leaveModifiedKey trigger . As part of the validation of a higher level. The condition for key validation is either one of the following: A key field has been modified, and the key has not yet been validated successfully. Validation is demanded for a key by setting $keycheck . Validation Process The process for validating a key is as follows: Uniface validates all fields that are part of the key. If the key is a compound key, Uniface checks that the key is complete. Uniface activates the validateKey trigger. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
